item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I knew something was up from the moment the other Foundation was noted as having a lot of political power over the Earth's global government

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I’m afraid I’m not a fan. -1

The worldbuilding here is rather dull and, truth be told, pointless to include. Compare my SCP-1322 (Glory Hole), a superficially similar concept that’s about the interaction between the Foundation and an alternate dimension’s counterpart agency. There, I intentionally eschewed including worldbuilding narrative except for the barest sketchy details.

This was for several reasons:

- I don’t think SCP articles are really the right medium for that kind of literature (you’re not going to be Tolkien in a short internet flash fiction piece).

- It violates the “show, don’t tell” principle of writing, and

- It’s really a distraction from what I think is really the thesis of your piece, which is really about the Foundation rather than being about the other guys.

I think your piece would have been stronger if you skipped the recitation of the worldbuilding.

Also, the dialogue is a bit too much in-your-face sloganeering. If what you’re trying to do is contrast the Foundstion’s ethos against that of these other guys, show, don’t tell- don’t have them explicitly debate their respective ethics, _show_ them actually engaging in contrasting ways and present your thesis they way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm with the other people who believe that this article fell a bit flat. I came into the article hoping that the alternate Earth wasn't just a strictly better version of Earth, but it basically ended up being exactly that. The article lists a lot of ways ARCRA's version of Earth is better than the baseline Earth and very few ways it's worse, which makes the O5's argument for freedom at the end of the article seem weak.

item.title: Good post!
item.content:encoded: Love it!

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Noting that the first image on this article is from NASA, and can be found on Wikimedia Commons, which is cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0). The other two images were created by the author of the article, and thus are cc-compliant as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love it. This article answers what happens when the Foundation decides to run the whole show. I enjoy how the other dimension has a distinct feel to it and ideological differences. The idea of anomalies being a part of everyday life puts some fascinating imagery in my head.

+1 easily

item.title: ARCRA stands for what?
item.content:encoded: So, what does the ARCRA acronym stand for? My guess is "Anomaly Recovery, Containment and Research Agency".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm sorry, but that quote is asinine. The entire point of societal laws is to give up freedoms in exchange for security. Agreeing not to steal, murder, or do other actions that are declared illegal. For example, no hunting species to extinction. Something that should have happened much earlier in human history.

The only thing in terms of freedom vs security should be "Is this a worth while trade?"

Ex. No screaming "Fire!" in a crowded and enclosed space for the sake of inciting panic.

item.title: Minor CSS Edits
item.content:encoded: Hey guys, thanks for all the responses! Just updating this to let everyone know that the top images have been changed into something a little more aesthetically pleasing. That is all for now. Thank you.

item.title: I love this article- it's one of the best I've ever read.
item.content:encoded:
You're lucky to have such writing talent. Any time I posted an article, it was instantly killed by downvotes.

But putting myself aside (or ceasing to be so selfish), your article was absolutely incredible. I just loved the way you organized everything, and the O5's line at the end sent shivers down my spine.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not directly opposed to the premise of the article, but I concur with other readers in that

A) The ethical discussion is overwhelmingly hamfisted and

B) The article, as a pulpit to speak from about the 'ideal' nature Our Foundation's universe, vehemently argues against the latter, which is unfortunately the worst possible scenario for something like this. The Foundation is an entity separated from the government but honest with it, supplies technology for all to benefit from, war is a thing of the past and society has entered a no-strings-attached post-scarcity utopia, without any intervention on the Foundation's behalf aside their breaking of the veil and donation of materiel.

It's essentially taking the 23rd century from Star Trek, comparing it Humanity circa 1999, and saying, 'well, we're better than you because… we're… free, I guess? While being saddled by disease, hunger, poverty, civil unrest, stagnation, bigotry, militarism, terrorism…'

I get the security vs. freedom argument. What freedoms did these people give up? Aside from the dialogue, that's it. It's very pretty and well-constructed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good job author, that was an amazing article. I have always wondered what the interactions between two different versions of the foundation would look like, and you have done it wonderfully. +1 for me.

One thing that confuses men is why Addendum 4994-X isn't Addendum 4994-D.

Other than that, you have a pretty good article here author.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I believe the O5 is a bit too moralistic or democratically so falling in lines with modern standards, however, it makes sense each O5 would have their own personality and reasons why they think the Foundation works.

I love the depiction of the ARCRA. They aren't innately hostile or anything. So long as you keep to your side of the fence and your side of the fence doesn't have a twelve-kilometer long demiurge controlling baseline humanity, we won't really feel the need to bother you as our priority is still to protect our world.

Which is sort of why I think the ARCRA representative's reasoning falls a bit flat in this regard? Simply because I think he would've come up with a better ending argument given this seems to be a conversation this version of the Foundation must've had before. What with infinite resources, multiple time/dimensional rifts, stargates becoming vacation destinations, etc.

I actually appreciate the darker aspects not mentioned in order to make this representation functional and at times, a better alternative. I mean. Darker aspects compared to what really given what our Foundation is capable of even with an Ethics Committee. It would be too moralistic and brow-beating for my taste. Leave them a mystery, an option, a hypothetical future policy or red button this Foundation may need to undergo.

They're the simulation that worked. ARCRA is a testament to "hindsight" potentially and that should be an existential fear the Foundation must grapple with.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey, a really good article, I'm going to upvote it!

"It seems you have already rated this content and your current rating is: +1."

*facepalms*

Either way, I liked it a lot :)

item.title: Response
item.content:encoded:
Lady Zero, this article is amazing. It's very interesting, and I wish you the best with your future articles. This one is just plain amazing. If you can write something as good as this, you could probably end up making another great SCP. I wish you glory.

- Communism Chicken

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeesh, the ARCRA have a dark side that makes the Foundation's look, not as dark.

ARCRA kind of reminds me of some interpretations of the Chaos Insurgency.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this a lot already, but a list of historical divergence points would improve it. Descriptions of those diversions may also hint at the nature of the conflict you're presenting.
+1 and waiting for any changes you might make.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I’m.. still not convinced by your argument. Ruling the world is not incompatible with serving it. It looks like the alternative Foundation does a better job in serving the world.
The argument that “not ruling = better” seems to be a rejection to all rulers. I mean, some of the rulers are good guys that truly serve their country and their prople. I would say that people definitely need such rulers - and if the Foundation can become one, why not?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am a sucker for alternate history. This gets an upvote, although there is one suggestion I have (less criticism and more something I wish it had). I'd love some sort of listing of where history diverged from baseline reality and some interesting events post-divergence. I also agree with some of the others that the conflict at the end seems abrupt and a lackluster ending for what is a fantastically executed build up. I want more. At the mention of the possible mission to steal some of their tech, my brain went down a rabbit hole of MTF logs, missions gone sideways, and a plot twist at the end where it's discovered that beneath the seemingly utopian facade, something evil is lurking. Aka all the cliches you could possibly use. This is why I don't write SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll just leave this here:
"Those who sacrifice freedom for security deserve neither."
-Benjamin Franklin.

The 05 was correct. We are better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't see how the history of this alternate civilisation relates to the ethical back-and-forth conversation at the end - since this is the whole point of the SCP, this is a big problem for me.

There are two separate issues going on within the final interview - the ethics of revealing anomalies vs. concealing them, and the ethics of an unaccountable organisation taking control of society for its own good. The former works fine in the article - though it's something that's come up many times on the site - and if that were all, I wouldn't have an objection, but the second part comes out of nowhere. Why does HC say the Foundation should take control of society? Nothing in the description of the SCP-4994 world indicates that they have placed ARCRA in charge or that ARCRA improved their society by manipulating things from behind the scenes. Considering that ARCRA considers the Foundation incompetents, why would they think the Foundation taking control would be a good thing (ok, I grant that HC has a different opinion to the rest of his colleagues, but still)?

The O5's response also doesn't scan. 'Accountability to the public' is not something that describes the Foundation in any way, shape or form, and given that the existence of ARCRA is public knowledge, it is almost certainly the more accountable organisation and far less vulnerable to authoritarian tendencies than the Foundation is. I would be on board with this if it were intended to show the hypocrisy of the Foundation administration, but that doesn't seem to be the case.

The ethical questions here are further undermined by the world of SCP-4994 apparently being superior to our own in every way possible - if this had come at a price of authoritarian control by ARCRA, as the final interview might imply, there would be some nuance to this, but like I said, I don't see how ARCRA could possibly be more authoritarian than the Foundation as presented here. The world described is like if you took 3003 and removed all the nasty parts of its society - why should we not follow this system, and in that case, where is the dilemma here? -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this is very good, ill be honest i skim most articles so the pacing wasn't a problem for me but you should definitely touch on that because for people who pay attention it's probably a big deal

the CSS you used for the title is ugly but I can look past that and just focus on the good stuff which is the article and especially that last line which is rEALLY GOOD

Im gonna go +1 regardless good jbo

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this. I think it touches on the whole theme of liberty vs. security pretty well, and the story leaves it ambiguous as to which side is truly right. It's just two similar organizations with differing ideas on what their roles in the world should be.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1. Totally agree with Crossync's point. I really like the tension between two similar but still alien cultures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1 for concept and delivery, though it's weak pacing-wise - it goes a bit fast, and the conflict is delivered right at the end. Whether or not ARCRA's suggestion is viable is an interesting pitch, but it just plops in there and then it's over.

I'd prefer more meat, let it stretch a little. Length can be tedious on some articles, but this is a big idea, and needs time to let the reader warm up to asking this question.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey guys, I'd like to thank all of you on this site so far. This is the 5th article that I've ever posted and hopefully it won't get downvoted to oblivion but I'm so glad I've had the chance to get creative and express myself among such talented writers.

And I'm always willing to learn, so please, give me your feedback! Did you like it? Did you hate it? Great!! I'm happy to have the chance to improve. Tell me what works and what didn't, and I'll apply that moving forward.

Thanks, all of you, so much. I'm glad to be a part of this community.
-Lady Zero
